standards.name: ADR Standard
standards.description: An Architecture Decision Record captures a significant decision: context, decision, rationale, and consequences. It preserves the "why" across time and across context resets.

standards.name: AI Attribution Standard
standards.description: AI-assisted development is the norm across all projects in this org. Commits should be **transparent** about which tool and model produced them, but **never pollute** GitHub contributor stats with fake accounts or co-authors that aren't real people.

standards.name: Auto-Commit GitOps Standard
standards.description: Automate commit metadata so that every commit is **signed**, **attributed**, and **dated correctly** without manual effort.

standards.name: SVG Badge Generator Standard
standards.description: Badges from shields.io URLs depend on GitHub's camo proxy, which times out on external fetches. Static SVGs committed to `docs/badges/` render instantly, have no external dependency, and never break.

standards.name: SVG Badge Generator Standard
standards.description: Badges from shields.io URLs depend on GitHub's camo proxy, which times out on external fetches. Static SVGs committed to `docs/badges/` render instantly, have no external dependency, and never break.

standards.name: Changelog Standard
standards.description: Use **[Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/2.0.0/)** format with **[Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html)**.

standards.name: CI Pipeline Standard
standards.description: Reference implementations live in each repo's `.github/workflows/`. See `.github/workflows/test.yml`:

standards.name: Commit Conventions Standard
standards.description: Branches follow the format: `type/description-in-kebab-case`

standards.name: CS Team Project Architecture Standard
standards.description: School team projects in computer science share a common problem: every group reinvents architectural decisions from scratch, wasting 2-3 weeks debating folder structure instead of writing code. This standard eliminates that waste.

standards.name: Git History Cleanup Standard
standards.description: Clean history when: - A **secret** was committed (API key, token, password, .env file) - **Private information** was committed (personal paths, emails, machine names) - A **file that shouldn't exist** was committed (binary, dependency, agent session) - A **large file** was committed that bloats the repo

standards.name: Git Identity Security Standard
standards.description: AI agents can hallucinate git identities (names, emails), causing signed commits to fail, attribution to break, and security policies to be bypassed. This standard locks down git's identity and signing configuration so only authorized identities can create commits.

standards.name: GitHub Repo Topics Standard
standards.description: GitHub repo topics are the primary **discoverability** mechanism. They appear in:

standards.name: .gitignore Standard — Whitelist ("gitaccept") Approach
standards.description: The traditional `.gitignore` uses a **blacklist**: allow everything, ignore specific patterns. The whitelist approach ("gitaccept") inverts this: **ignore everything, accept specific patterns**.

standards.name: Language Standard — American English
standards.description: All documentation, commit messages, code comments, and user-facing text **must use American English** spelling and conventions.

standards.name: License Standard
standards.description: All public repos should use the **MIT License** unless there's a specific reason to choose otherwise.

standards.name: Link Rot Standard
standards.description: Documentation links degrade over time — domains expire, pages are restructured, projects are archived. Automated link checking prevents stale references from accumulating in READMEs, documentation, and code comments.

standards.name: Naming Conventions Standard
standards.description: - kebab-case: `agentic-workflows`, `ithmb-codec`, `bus-hop`, `cs-notes` - No underscores. No uppercase. No spaces. - Short but meaningful — avoid abbreviations unless well-known (CS, UI, API) - **Validated:** 76% of the top 100 most-starred GitHub repos use kebab-case. snake_case is completely absent (0%).

standards.name: Path Agnosticism Standard
standards.description: All scripts and configuration in this repository MUST be **path-agnostic** — they must work on any machine without modification. Absolute paths, user-specific paths, and machine-specific assumptions break portability.

standards.name: README Standard
standards.description: The title is an H1 heading wrapped in a centered `<div>` tag, followed by the badge row and summary line inside the same centered block:

standards.name: Repository Structure Standard
standards.description: All repos SHOULD have these directories at the top level:

standards.name: Safe Wrappers Standard
standards.description: AI agents and shell automation invoke git and gh commands generically. Without guardrails, a commit with `--no-verify` can bypass hook enforcement, a `--force` push can rewrite remote history, and an unverified identity can corrupt attribution. Safe wrappers intercept these commands to enforce policy at the shell level.

standards.name: Self-Consistency Standard
standards.description: The standards repository must be self-consistent — it must practice what it preaches. Every standard defined in this repo applies to this repo itself. If a standard cannot be satisfied by this repo (e.g., because the standard is a meta-standard about a type of project this repo isn't), it MUST be documented as an explicit exception.

standards.name: Secrets Management Standard
standards.description: Keep secrets out of git history. Encrypt `.env` files at rest, store the encrypted version in git, and decrypt only at runtime. This prevents credential leaks while keeping secrets deployable.

standards.name: SVG Screenshot Standard
standards.description: Screenshots in READMEs are currently PNG/JPEG — raster images that don't scale, look blurry on retina displays, and can't adapt to dark mode. SVG screenshots solve all three problems.

standards.name: Tool Version Standard
standards.description: Keep development tool versions consistent across machines with a version-manager config file. This ensures every contributor and CI runner uses the same toolchain.

standards.name: Secrets Management Standard
standards.description: Keep secrets out of git history. Encrypt `.env` files at rest, store the encrypted version in git, and decrypt only at runtime. This prevents credential leaks while keeping secrets deployable.
